title: Network Latency Monitoring (Agent-to-Server)
dataSources: `index=thousandeyes_metrics sourcetype=cisco:thousandeyes:metric` from the Tests Stream — Metrics input in `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` (Splunkbase 7719). The app creates an OpenTelemetry stream in ThousandEyes that pushes Agent-to-Server test results to Splunk via HEC. The OTel v2 metric `network.latency` reports the maximum round-trip time in **seconds** (not milliseconds — the v1→v2 migration changed units). Common resource attributes: `thousandeyes.test.type` (`agent-to-server`), `thousandeyes.test.name`, `thousandeyes.source.agent.name`, `thousandeyes.source.agent.location`, `server.address`, `server.port`, `thousandeyes.permalink`. Default stream interval: 60 seconds per test round. Volume: ~1.5 KB per agent-target pair per round ≈ 2 MB/agent-target/day at 1-minute rounds. A deployment with 20 agents testing 10 targets produces ~400 MB/day for this sourcetype alone.
app: `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` — Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk ([Splunkbase 7719](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/7719)), Cisco Supported. Install on Search Heads (dashboards, knowledge objects, macros) AND on the Heavy Forwarder or single-instance Splunk that will receive HEC data. Do NOT install on Universal Forwarders — UFs cannot receive HEC streams or run the OAuth token refresh input.
spl:
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="agent-to-server"
| stats avg(network.latency) as avg_latency_s max(network.latency) as max_latency_s by thousandeyes.source.agent.name, server.address
| eval avg_latency_ms=round(avg_latency_s*1000,1), max_latency_ms=round(max_latency_s*1000,1)
| where avg_latency_ms > 100
| sort -avg_latency_ms
description: Surfaces Agent-to-Server test results showing round-trip latency from each ThousandEyes Cloud or Enterprise Agent to its configured target server, ranked by worst average latency first. The search converts the OTel v2 metric (reported in seconds) to milliseconds and flags any agent-target pair averaging above 100 ms — a threshold where interactive applications begin to feel sluggish and real-time protocols degrade noticeably.
value: Latency spikes on a specific agent-to-target path almost always mean the problem sits in an ISP hop, a peering exchange, or a congested WAN segment — not in the application itself. Catching the spike in Splunk before users escalate lets the NOC correlate with ThousandEyes Path Visualization (UC-5.9.33) to pinpoint the exact hop, open a ticket with the ISP while the evidence is fresh, and communicate an ETA to affected business units. Over weeks, the trend line reveals chronic paths that need circuit upgrades, SD-WAN policy changes, or failover tuning — turning reactive firefighting into planned network optimization.
implementation: Install `ta_cisco_thousandeyes` (Splunkbase 7719) on the Search Head and Heavy Forwarder. Complete the OAuth 2.0 device code authentication flow. Create a HEC token named `thousandeyes` and the `thousandeyes_metrics` index. Enable the Tests Stream — Metrics input, selecting the Agent-to-Server tests to stream. Verify the `stream_index` macro resolves to `index=thousandeyes_metrics`. Schedule the search every 15 minutes over the last 1 hour, with alert trigger on results where `avg_latency_ms > 100`, throttled by `thousandeyes.source.agent.name` + `server.address` for 4 hours.
visualization: (1) Sortable table: agent name, server address, avg latency (ms), max latency (ms) — sorted worst-first so the most degraded paths are immediately visible. (2) Single value tiles: count of paths exceeding 100 ms (red threshold ≥ 1), fleet-wide average latency (yellow > 50 ms). (3) Timechart overlay: `| timechart span=5m avg(network.latency) as avg_latency_s by thousandeyes.source.agent.name` showing latency trends per agent over 24 hours — useful for spotting time-of-day patterns tied to ISP congestion windows. (4) Drilldown: click an agent-target row to open the ThousandEyes permalink URL (`thousandeyes.permalink` field) in a new tab for Path Visualization and hop-by-hop analysis.

references.title: ThousandEyes OTel v2 Data Model — Metrics Migration (v1 to v2)

knownFalsePositives:
**ISP peak-hour congestion.** Latency regularly rises 20–50% during business hours on shared last-mile or transit links (typically 08:00–10:00 and 16:00–18:00 local time). Distinguish from a real fault by checking whether the elevation affects all agents in the same metro or ISP, and whether it follows a consistent daily pattern over 7+ days. Tune by raising the threshold to 150 ms for known congested paths, or by adding a `| where avg_latency_ms > perc95_baseline` comparison against a saved baseline lookup.

**BGP route change transients.** During BGP convergence (path change, peer flap, or maintenance), latency can spike for 1–3 test rounds as traffic takes a suboptimal path. Distinguish by correlating with UC-5.9.9 (BGP Path Change Trending) — if a path change and latency spike overlap within a 5-minute window and latency recovers, it was convergence. Suppress by requiring two consecutive rounds above threshold before alerting.

**Cloud provider maintenance windows.** AWS, Azure, and GCP periodically perform network maintenance that temporarily increases latency to targets hosted in those clouds. Distinguish by checking cloud provider status pages and correlating with UC-5.9.31 (Multi-Cloud Network Performance). Suppress by maintaining a `cloud_maintenance_windows` lookup and excluding matching time ranges.

**Agent host resource contention.** Enterprise Agents running on VMs with insufficient CPU or memory can report inflated latency due to scheduling delays in the agent process itself, not actual network delay. Distinguish by comparing Cloud Agent results to the same target — if Cloud Agents show normal latency while the Enterprise Agent shows high latency, the agent host is the bottleneck. Fix by allocating dedicated vCPU and memory to the agent VM.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk (`ta_cisco_thousandeyes`, [Splunkbase 7719](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/7719)) installed on Search Heads (dashboards, macros, field extractions) AND on the Heavy Forwarder or single-instance Splunk that receives HEC data. Do NOT deploy to Universal Forwarders — they cannot receive HEC pushes or run the OAuth token refresh modular input.
• **ThousandEyes account** with Essentials, Advantage, or Premier tier. The Streaming API (OpenTelemetry export) requires an account-level entitlement — verify under **Account Settings → Usage & Billing → OpenTelemetry Streaming**. Free-trial accounts cannot create streams.
• **At least one Agent-to-Server test** configured in ThousandEyes targeting the servers or endpoints you want to monitor. Cloud Agents (ThousandEyes-hosted, globally distributed) work out of the box; Enterprise Agents (customer-deployed) require the agent software installed on a Linux VM, Docker container, or supported appliance. Confirm the test is active and collecting data in **Cloud & Enterprise Agents → Test Settings**.
• **OAuth 2.0 authentication** completed. The app uses an interactive device code flow — no API keys or passwords. The user visits a verification URL in their browser to authorize. Tokens are encrypted and stored in Splunk's credential store automatically. A `thousandeyes_refresh_tokens` modular input runs weekly to keep tokens valid.
• **HEC enabled** with a valid (non-self-signed) SSL certificate. ThousandEyes pushes data TO Splunk via HEC — Splunk does not poll. On Splunk Cloud, HEC is enabled by default at `https://http-inputs-{stack}.splunkcloud.com:443`. On Splunk Enterprise, enable HEC in **Settings → Data Inputs → HTTP Event Collector → Global Settings → Enabled**. The ThousandEyes platform must be able to reach this endpoint over the public internet (or via a proxy).
• **HEC token** named `thousandeyes` (or custom name) with `thousandeyes_metrics` as an allowed index. Create via the app's setup script or manually in **Settings → Data Inputs → HTTP Event Collector → New Token**.
• **Splunk 9.1+** or Splunk Cloud. CIM Add-on 6.0.1+ recommended but not required (ThousandEyes OTel metrics do not map to standard CIM models).
• **Network access:** ThousandEyes Streaming API endpoints (egress from ThousandEyes cloud) must reach the Splunk HEC URL on port 443 (Cloud) or 8088 (Enterprise). If a firewall or proxy sits between them, allow the ThousandEyes IP ranges documented at https://docs.thousandeyes.com/product-documentation/global-vantage-points/thousandeyes-ip-ranges.
• **Splunk role:** users running this search need `srchIndexesAllowed` including `thousandeyes_metrics`. Create a custom role (`network_observer`) rather than granting `admin`.
• **License headroom:** the `cisco:thousandeyes:metric` sourcetype generates ~1.5 KB per agent-target pair per test round. A deployment with 20 agents × 10 targets × 1440 rounds/day (1-minute interval) ≈ **400 MB/day**. Scale linearly with agent count × target count × round frequency. See the app's sizing guidance for multi-test-type estimates.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
On the Heavy Forwarder (or single-instance Splunk), complete the OAuth flow:
1. Navigate to **Apps → Cisco ThousandEyes App for Splunk → Configuration → Account** and click **Add**.
2. The app initiates an OAuth 2.0 device code flow and displays a **verification URL** and **user code** in the browser.
3. Visit the URL, enter the code, and authorize the Splunk integration with your ThousandEyes account.
4. Tokens are stored encrypted in `passwords.conf`. The `thousandeyes_refresh_tokens` input (enabled by default) auto-refreshes tokens weekly.

Then enable the Tests Stream — Metrics input:
1. Navigate to **Inputs → Create New Input → Tests Stream — Metrics**.
2. Configure:

| Setting | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Account | (the OAuth account you just created) |
| Account Group | (select your ThousandEyes account group) |
| HEC Token | `thousandeyes` |
| Index | `thousandeyes_metrics` |
| Test Tags | (optional — filter by ThousandEyes test tags to stream only specific tests) |
| Test Type | Cloud & Enterprise Agent Tests |

3. Click **Save**. The app calls the ThousandEyes Streaming API to create a stream object in ThousandEyes that pushes OTel metrics to the configured HEC endpoint.
4. The app's `stream_index` macro defaults to `index=thousandeyes_metrics`. Verify: **Settings → Advanced Search → Search Macros → stream_index**. If you used a different index name, update the macro.

Verification: wait 2–3 minutes for the first test round to complete and stream, then run:
```spl
index=thousandeyes_metrics sourcetype="cisco:thousandeyes:metric" thousandeyes.test.type="agent-to-server" earliest=-10m
| stats count by thousandeyes.source.agent.name, server.address
```
You should see one row per agent-target pair. Compare `| stats dc(thousandeyes.source.agent.name) as agents dc(server.address) as targets` to the agent and target counts in **ThousandEyes → Cloud & Enterprise Agents → Test Settings** for your Agent-to-Server tests.

Expected fields in each event: `network.latency` (float, seconds), `network.loss` (float, percentage 0–100), `network.jitter` (float, milliseconds), `thousandeyes.test.type` (string: `agent-to-server`), `thousandeyes.test.name` (string), `thousandeyes.source.agent.name` (string), `thousandeyes.source.agent.location` (string), `server.address` (string — IP or hostname), `server.port` (integer), `thousandeyes.permalink` (URL to ThousandEyes test view for this round).

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
```spl
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="agent-to-server"
| stats avg(network.latency) as avg_latency_s max(network.latency) as max_latency_s by thousandeyes.source.agent.name, server.address
| eval avg_latency_ms=round(avg_latency_s*1000,1), max_latency_ms=round(max_latency_s*1000,1)
| where avg_latency_ms > 100
| sort -avg_latency_ms
```

**Understanding this SPL**

`stream_index` — expands to `index=thousandeyes_metrics` (the app-defined macro). This scopes the search to only ThousandEyes streaming metrics.

`thousandeyes.test.type="agent-to-server"` — filters to Agent-to-Server network tests only. Other test types (HTTP Server, DNS, BGP, Page Load) use different metric names and are covered by other UCs in this subcategory.

`stats avg(network.latency) as avg_latency_s max(network.latency) as max_latency_s by ...` — computes the average and maximum round-trip time for each agent-target pair over the search window. Using `avg()` smooths transient spikes (a single slow round amid 59 normal ones); using `max()` alongside captures the worst-case for SLA reporting. The `by` clause splits on agent name and target server so each network path gets its own row.

`eval avg_latency_ms=round(avg_latency_s*1000,1)` — the OTel v2 data model reports `network.latency` in **seconds** (e.g., `0.045` = 45 ms). This is a deliberate change from v1 which used milliseconds. The eval converts to milliseconds for human readability. This is critical: without the conversion, a threshold of `100` would mean 100 seconds, not 100 ms.

`where avg_latency_ms > 100` — 100 ms is the threshold where interactive applications (SSH, RDP, web UIs) begin to feel noticeably laggy and where VoIP quality degrades below MOS 4.0. For your environment, tune this based on expected baseline latency: cross-continental paths may have 150–200 ms baselines legitimately, while same-datacenter paths should be < 5 ms. Consider maintaining a `thousandeyes_latency_baselines` lookup with per-path thresholds.

`sort -avg_latency_ms` — worst-first ordering so the NOC immediately sees the most degraded path at the top of the table.

**Scheduling:** cron `*/15 * * * *`, time range `-1h to now`. Trigger on "Number of results > 0". Throttle suppression on `thousandeyes.source.agent.name` + `server.address` for 4 hours so the same degraded path doesn't re-page during an ongoing incident.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) **Cross-reference ThousandEyes UI.** In ThousandEyes, navigate to **Cloud & Enterprise Agents → Views → Agent to Server** and select the same time window as your Splunk search. Pick three agent-target pairs: one with low latency (< 20 ms), one mid-range (50–80 ms), and one known high-latency path. In Splunk, run:
```spl
`stream_index` thousandeyes.test.type="agent-to-server" thousandeyes.source.agent.name="<agent>" server.address="<target>" earliest=-1h
| stats avg(network.latency) as avg_s | eval avg_ms=round(avg_s*1000,1)
```
The values should match within 1–2 ms (timing differences from when the round completed vs when the stream delivered the event).

(b) **Count parity.** Compare `| stats dc(thousandeyes.source.agent.name) as agents, dc(server.address) as targets` in Splunk with the agent and test counts in ThousandEyes **Test Settings**. If Splunk shows fewer agents, check whether some tests were excluded by the Test Tags filter when you created the stream input, or whether the stream was scoped to a specific account group that doesn't include all agents.

(c) **Field completeness.** Run `| fieldsummary` on a 15-minute window. All key fields (`network.latency`, `thousandeyes.source.agent.name`, `server.address`, `thousandeyes.test.name`, `thousandeyes.permalink`) should show non-zero distinct counts. If `network.latency` is entirely absent, the stream may be delivering v1 metric names (`net.metrics.latency`) — check your ThousandEyes OTel data version setting under **Integrations → Streams → Edit → Data Model Version**.

(d) **Ingest cadence.** `| timechart span=1m count` should show a regular pattern with one event per agent-target pair per test round. Gaps indicate stream delivery failures — check HEC health (Step 5).

(e) **Unit verification.** Confirm values are in seconds by spotting a low-latency path (e.g., same-datacenter). If `network.latency` reads `0.003`, that's 3 ms — correct for v2. If it reads `3.0`, you're on v1 (milliseconds) and the eval conversion will produce incorrect values (3000 ms). Update the ThousandEyes stream to v2 or adjust the eval accordingly.

Step 4 — Operationalize
**Dashboard** (recommended layout — "ThousandEyes — Network Latency Overview"):
• Row 1 — Single value tiles: "Paths > 100 ms" (red threshold ≥ 1, drilldown to filtered table), "Fleet avg latency" (gauge, yellow > 50 ms, red > 100 ms), "Unreachable paths" (count where `network.loss` = 100, links to UC-5.9.2).
• Row 2 — Sortable table: `thousandeyes.source.agent.name` | `server.address` | `avg_latency_ms` | `max_latency_ms` | `thousandeyes.source.agent.location` — sorted worst-first. Drilldown: click a row to open the `thousandeyes.permalink` URL in a new tab, landing directly in the ThousandEyes Path Visualization for that test round.
• Row 3 — Timechart: `| timechart span=5m avg(network.latency) as avg_s by thousandeyes.source.agent.name | eval avg_ms=avg_s*1000` showing latency trends per agent over 24 hours. Useful for identifying time-of-day congestion patterns (ISP rush hours) vs sustained degradation (circuit fault).
• Row 4 — Geographic map (if agent locations are available): plot agents by `thousandeyes.source.agent.location` with color-coded markers (green < 50 ms, yellow 50–100 ms, red > 100 ms) for a global latency heatmap.
• Time-picker presets: "Last 1 hour" (incident triage), "Last 24 hours" (daily review), "Last 7 days" (trend analysis).

**Alerting:**
• Splunk On-Call / PagerDuty: low-urgency on first trigger per path, high-urgency if the same path re-fires within 4 hours or if > 3 paths simultaneously exceed the threshold (suggests an upstream transit issue). Include the `thousandeyes.permalink` URL in the alert payload so the on-call engineer can jump directly to ThousandEyes Path Visualization.
• Slack/Teams notification to `#network-ops` for visibility.

**Runbook** (owner: Network Operations on-call):
1. Open the alert details. Note the agent name, target server, and average latency.
2. Click the `thousandeyes.permalink` URL to open the ThousandEyes Path Visualization. Identify which hop in the path shows the highest per-hop latency or loss.
3. If the degraded hop is within your network (internal router/switch IP), SSH to the device and check interface utilization (`show interface <x>` for Cisco, `show interfaces queue` for QoS drops). Correlate with UC-5.1.2 (Interface Utilization) if you have syslog data from that device.
4. If the degraded hop is in an ISP or transit network, open a ticket with the ISP. Include the ThousandEyes permalink as evidence — it shows the exact hop IP, latency, and loss at the time of the alert.
5. If multiple agents in the same metro show elevated latency to the same target, the issue is likely peering or transit — check the ThousandEyes Internet Outages map under **Internet Insights** for known outages affecting that ISP.
6. If only one agent is affected while others to the same target are normal, the issue is local to that agent's network path. Check: agent host health (CPU, memory), local network equipment, last-mile circuit.
7. If latency elevated but stable (not spiking), check whether a BGP path change occurred (UC-5.9.9). A longer AS path after a route change is a common cause of sustained latency increase.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **No events at all in `thousandeyes_metrics`** — The most common cause: the HEC endpoint is unreachable from the ThousandEyes platform. ThousandEyes pushes data TO Splunk (unlike most TAs which poll). Verify: (1) HEC is enabled globally in Splunk, (2) the HEC token exists and is enabled, (3) the HEC URL is correct — on Splunk Cloud, it must be `https://http-inputs-{stack}.splunkcloud.com:443`, NOT the search head URL. On Enterprise, it must be `https://{hf-hostname}:8088`. (4) The SSL certificate on the HEC endpoint is valid and non-self-signed — ThousandEyes rejects self-signed certs. (5) No firewall or WAF is blocking inbound connections from ThousandEyes IP ranges. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=HttpInputDataHandler` for HEC connection attempts.

• **Events arriving but `network.latency` field is missing** — You may be receiving v1 OTel data (field name `net.metrics.latency` instead of `network.latency`). Check: `index=thousandeyes_metrics | stats count by metric_name | head 20`. If you see `net.metrics.*` prefix, the stream is using the v1 data model. Fix: in ThousandEyes **Integrations → Streams → Edit the stream → set Data Model Version to v2**. Note that v1 uses milliseconds; v2 uses seconds — the SPL eval depends on v2 units.

• **Fewer agent-target pairs than expected** — The stream input may have been filtered by test tags when created. Check in the Splunk app: **Inputs → Tests Stream — Metrics → Edit** and verify the Test Tags filter. Also check that the OAuth account's account group includes all the tests you expect. If tests belong to different account groups, create separate stream inputs per group.

• **Latency values seem 1000× too high or too low** — Unit mismatch between OTel v1 and v2. v1 reports `net.metrics.latency` in milliseconds; v2 reports `network.latency` in seconds. If your eval multiplies by 1000 but the data is already in milliseconds, you'll see values like 45000 ms (should be 45 ms). Check the raw event: `index=thousandeyes_metrics thousandeyes.test.type="agent-to-server" | head 1 | table network.latency net.metrics.latency`. Whichever field is populated tells you the version.

• **Gaps in timechart (missing test rounds)** — Check ThousandEyes test status: **Test Settings → select the test → Status** should show "Active". If the test is paused or the agent is offline, no data streams. Also check HEC queue health: `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=HttpInputDataHandler "queue"` for signs of queue backup or rejection.

• **OAuth token expired — no new data** — The `thousandeyes_refresh_tokens` modular input should auto-refresh tokens. If it's disabled or failed, the OAuth token expires and the stream stops. Check: **Inputs → thousandeyes_refresh_tokens** must be Enabled. Check logs: `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ExecProcessor "thousandeyes"` for refresh failures. If the token cannot be refreshed, re-run the OAuth device code flow (Configuration → Account → Re-authenticate).

• **Alerts firing during known maintenance** — Maintain a `thousandeyes_maintenance_windows` lookup with `start_time`, `end_time`, `scope` (agent name or target pattern). Add `| lookup thousandeyes_maintenance_windows ... | where NOT in_maintenance=1` to the alert search, or use alert suppression windows in your incident management tool.

• **Dashboard shows "No results found"** — Verify the `stream_index` macro is correctly defined: **Settings → Advanced Search → Search Macros**. The macro should expand to `index=thousandeyes_metrics`. If you used a custom index name during setup, update the macro. Also verify your Splunk user role has `srchIndexesAllowed` including `thousandeyes_metrics`.
grandmaExplanation: We keep an eye on how long it takes our tests to reach important servers, so we notice a slowing internet path before people start complaining about slow apps or choppy calls.
